note: Stoic quotes drawn from public-domain translations (George Long, Richard Gummere, Elizabeth Carter, P.E. Matheson, Cora Lutz and others), lightly modernized and in places condensed. Extend by appending entries; the site picks quote by day-of-year modulo list length.

quotes.text: You could leave life right now. Let that determine what you do and say and think.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: It is not death that a man should fear, but never beginning to live.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Think of yourself as dead. You have lived your life. Now take what is left and live it properly.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Do not act as if you had ten thousand years to live. While you live, while it is in your power, be good.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Perfection of character is this: to live each day as if it were your last, without frenzy, without apathy, without pretense.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Soon you will have forgotten everything; soon everything will have forgotten you.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: When you arise in the morning, think of what a precious privilege it is to be alive, to breathe, to think, to enjoy, to love.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Waste no more time arguing about what a good man should be. Be one.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: The universe is change; our life is what our thoughts make it.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Loss is nothing else than change, and change is nature's delight.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Despise not death, but welcome it, for nature wills it like all else.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Death, like birth, is a mystery of nature; there is nothing in it to be afraid of.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Do every act of your life as though it were the very last act of your life.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: In the morning when you rise unwillingly, let this thought be present: I am rising to the work of a human being.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Confine yourself to the present.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Every man's life lies within the present; the past is spent and done with, and the future is uncertain.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Time is a river of passing events, and strong is its current; no sooner is a thing brought to sight than it is swept by, and another takes its place.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Consider how quickly all things are dissolved and resolved.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: All is ephemeral, both what remembers and what is remembered.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Whatever happens to you has been waiting to happen since the beginning of time.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Nothing happens to any man that he is not formed by nature to bear.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: The best revenge is not to be like your enemy.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: If it is not right, do not do it; if it is not true, do not say it.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Very little is needed to make a happy life; it is all within yourself, in your way of thinking.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: You have power over your mind, not outside events. Realize this, and you will find strength.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: The happiness of your life depends upon the quality of your thoughts.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Look back over the past, with its changing empires that rose and fell, and you can foresee the future too.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: How much time he gains who does not look to see what his neighbor says or does or thinks.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Be like the promontory against which the waves continually break; it stands firm and tames the fury of the water around it.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: The object of life is not to be on the side of the majority, but to escape finding oneself in the ranks of the insane.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Accept the things to which fate binds you, and love the people with whom fate brings you together, and do so with all your heart.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: The impediment to action advances action. What stands in the way becomes the way.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Choose not to be harmed and you will not feel harmed. Do not feel harmed and you have not been.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: It is in your power to withdraw yourself whenever you desire. Perfect tranquility within consists in the good ordering of the mind.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Nowhere can man find a quieter or more untroubled retreat than in his own soul.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: A man's worth is no greater than the worth of his ambitions.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Everything that happens is either endurable or not. If it is endurable, then endure it. Stop complaining.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: If you are distressed by anything external, the pain is not due to the thing itself but to your estimate of it; and this you have the power to revoke at any moment.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Death hangs over you. While you live, while it is in your power, be good.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: He who has seen present things has seen all, both everything that has taken place from all eternity and everything that will be for time without end.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Short then is the time which every man lives, and small the nook of the earth where he lives.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Everything is only for a day, both that which remembers and that which is remembered.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Observe constantly that all things take place by change, and accustom yourself to consider that the nature of the universe loves nothing so much as to change things.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Whatever may happen to you, it was prepared for you from all eternity.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Adapt yourself to the things among which your lot has been cast, and love sincerely the fellow creatures with whom destiny has ordained that you shall live.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Do not disturb yourself by picturing your life as a whole; do not assemble in your mind the many and varied troubles which have come to you in the past and will come again in the future.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Remember that man lives only in the present, in this fleeting instant; all the rest of his life is either past and gone, or not yet revealed.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Never let the future disturb you. You will meet it, if you have to, with the same weapons of reason which today arm you against the present.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: How ridiculous and how strange to be surprised at anything which happens in life.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: That which has died falls not out of the universe. If it stays here, it also changes here, and is dissolved into its proper parts, which are elements of the universe and of yourself.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Do not then consider life a thing of any value. For look at the immensity of time behind you, and to the time which is before you, another boundless space.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: In this flowing stream, on which there is no abiding, what is there of the things which hurry by on which a man would set a high price?
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: He who does not know what the world is, does not know where he is. He who does not know for what purpose the world exists, does not know who he is.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Think of the universal substance, of which you have a very small portion; and of universal time, of which a short and indivisible interval has been assigned to you.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Everything which belongs to the body is a stream, and what belongs to the soul is a dream and vapor; life is a warfare and a stranger's sojourn, and after-fame is oblivion.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: What then is that which is able to conduct a man? One thing and only one: philosophy.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Do the things external which fall upon you distract you? Give yourself time to learn something new and good, and cease to be whirled around.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Let no act be done without a purpose, nor otherwise than according to the perfect principles of art.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Men seek retreats for themselves: houses in the country, seashores, and mountains. But this is altogether a mark of the most common sort of men, for it is in your power whenever you choose to retire into yourself.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: This is the chief thing: be not perturbed, for all things are according to the nature of the universal.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Take away your opinion, and there is taken away the complaint. Take away the complaint, and the hurt is gone.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: That which is not good for the swarm, neither is it good for the bee.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Let it make no difference to you whether you are cold or warm, if you are doing your duty; and whether you are drowsy or satisfied with sleep.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: A cucumber is bitter: throw it away. There are briars in the road: turn aside from them. This is enough. Do not add, why were such things made in the world?
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Be not ashamed to be helped, for it is your business to do your duty like a soldier in the assault on a town.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Judge every word and deed which are according to nature to be fit for you, and be not diverted by the blame which follows.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: If it is good to say or do anything, do not consider it unworthy of you to be said or done.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: The soul becomes dyed with the color of its thoughts.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Dig within. Within is the wellspring of good; and it is always ready to bubble up, if you just dig.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Look within. Within is the fountain of good, and it will ever bubble up, if you will ever dig.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: No man can rob us of our free will.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Receive wealth or prosperity without arrogance; and be ready to let it go.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: It is not fit that I should give myself pain, for I have never intentionally given pain even to another.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: To live happily is an inward power of the soul.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Life is neither good nor evil, but only a place for good and evil.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Today I have got out of all trouble, or rather I have cast out all trouble, for it was not outside, but within and in my opinions.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Remember how long you have been putting off these things, and how often you have received an opportunity from the gods, and yet do not use it.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: You must now at last perceive of what universe you are a part, and that a limit of time is fixed for you, which if you do not use for clearing away the clouds from your mind, it will go and you will go, and it will never return.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Every moment think steadily as a Roman and a man to do what you have in hand with perfect and simple dignity, and with feeling of affection, and freedom, and justice.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Do the things which nature demands of a man while you are still able, and while the power is still yours.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Since it is possible that you may depart from life this very moment, regulate every act and thought accordingly.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: No man loses any other life than the one he now lives, nor lives any other life than the one he now loses.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: The longest-lived and the shortest-lived man, when they come to die, lose one and the same thing: the present.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: For a man cannot lose either the past or the future; for what a man has not, how can anyone take this from him?
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Though you should be going to live three thousand years, and as many times ten thousand years, still remember that no man loses any other life than this which he now lives.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: All things from eternity are of like forms and come round in a circle.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Consider that before long you will be nobody and nowhere, nor will any of the things exist which you now see, nor any of those who are now living.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: All things are changing; and you yourself are in continuous mutation and in a manner in continuous destruction, and the whole universe too.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Alexander the Great and his mule driver both died and the same thing happened to both: they were absorbed alike into the life force of the world, or dissolved alike into atoms.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Do not act as if you were going to live ten thousand years. Death hangs over you. While you live, while it is in your power, be good.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Pass then through this little space of time conformably to nature, and end your journey in content, just as an olive falls off when it is ripe, blessing nature who produced it, and thanking the tree on which it grew.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: What is death? A cessation of the impressions through the senses, and of the pulling of the strings which move the appetites, and of the discursive movements of the thoughts, and of the service to the flesh.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: It is a shame for the soul to be first to give way in this life, when your body does not give way.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Take heed not to be transformed into a Caesar, not to be dipped in the purple dye; for it does happen. Keep yourself therefore simple, good, pure, grave, free from affectation.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Reverence the gods, and help men. Short is life.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Whatever this is that I am, it is a little flesh and breath, and the ruling part.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: As surgeons keep their instruments ready for cases which suddenly require their skill, so keep your principles ready for understanding things divine and human.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: The art of life is more like the wrestler's art than the dancer's, in respect of this: that it should stand ready and firm to meet onsets which are sudden and unexpected.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Let not future things disturb you, for you will come to them, if it shall be necessary, having with you the same reason which you now use for present things.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Love the little trade which you have learned, and be content with it.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: He who loves fame considers another man's activity to be his own good; and he who loves pleasure, his own sensations; but he who has understanding considers his own acts to be his own good.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: It is in our power to have no opinion about a thing, and not to be disturbed in our soul; for things themselves have no natural power to form our judgments.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: If you work at that which is before you, following right reason seriously, vigorously, calmly, you will live happy. And there is no man who is able to prevent this.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Remember that neither the future nor the past pains you, but only the present.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Everywhere and at all times it is in your power piously to acquiesce in your present condition, and to behave justly to those who are about you.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Nothing is more wretched than a man who traverses everything in a round, and pries into the things beneath the earth, and seeks by conjecture what is in the minds of his neighbors, without perceiving that it is sufficient to attend to the spirit within him.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Give yourself time to learn something new and good, and cease to be whirled around.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Be cheerful, and seek no external help, nor the tranquility which others give. A man then must stand erect, not be kept erect by others.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Whatever anyone does or says, I must be good; just as if the gold, or the emerald, or the purple were always saying, whatever anyone does or says, I must be an emerald and keep my color.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Life is short. That is all there is to say. Get what you can from the present, thoughtfully, justly.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: True good fortune is what you make for yourself. Good fortune: good character, good intentions, and good actions.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Begin at once to live, and count each separate day as a separate life.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: It is not that we have a short time to live, but that we waste a lot of it.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Life is long, if you know how to use it.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: They lose the day in expectation of the night, and the night in fear of the dawn.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: While we are postponing, life speeds by.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Nothing is ours, except time.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: We should count each day as a separate life.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Let us prepare our minds as if we had come to the very end of life. Let us postpone nothing. Let us balance life's books each day.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The one who puts the finishing touches on their life each day is never short of time.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: You act like mortals in all that you fear, and like immortals in all that you desire.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: How late it is to begin to live just when we must cease to live.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: It takes the whole of life to learn how to live, and, what will perhaps make you wonder more, it takes the whole of life to learn how to die.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: He who fears death will never do anything worthy of a man who is alive.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Study death always, that you may fear it never.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Death is not an evil. What is it then? The one law mankind has that is free of all discrimination.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Wherever you look, there is the end of evils. You see that yawning precipice? It leads to liberty.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: No man enjoys the true taste of life but he who is willing and ready to quit it.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: We die every day. Every day a little of our life is taken from us; even when we are growing, our life is on the wane.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The final hour when we cease to exist does not itself bring death; it merely of itself completes the death process.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Whatever can happen at any time can happen today.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Nothing is so wretched or foolish as to anticipate misfortunes. What madness it is to be expecting evil before it comes.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: We suffer more often in imagination than in reality.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: If you wish to fear nothing, consider that everything is to be feared.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The mind that is anxious about future events is miserable.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: True happiness is to enjoy the present, without anxious dependence upon the future.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: He suffers more than necessary, who suffers before it is necessary.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Luck is what happens when preparation meets opportunity.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: It is a rough road that leads to the heights of greatness.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Fire tests gold, misfortune tests brave men.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Difficulties strengthen the mind, as labor does the body.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: A gem cannot be polished without friction, nor a man perfected without trials.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: No man is more unhappy than he who never faces adversity. For he is not permitted to prove himself.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: I judge you unfortunate because you have never lived through misfortune. You have passed through life without an opponent; no one can ever know what you are capable of, not even you.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: It is not the man who has too little, but the man who craves more, that is poor.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Until we have begun to go without them, we fail to realize how unnecessary many things are. We have been using them not because we needed them but because we had them.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: It is in no man's power to have whatever he wants; but he has it in his power not to wish for what he has not, and cheerfully make the most of the things that do come his way.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: That man is richest whose pleasures are cheapest.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Set aside a certain number of days during which you shall be content with the scantiest and cheapest fare, with coarse and rough dress, saying to yourself the while: is this the condition that I feared?
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: He is a great man who uses earthenware dishes as if they were silver; but he is equally great who uses silver as if it were earthenware.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Wealth is the slave of a wise man and the master of a fool.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: If a man knows not to which port he sails, no wind is favorable.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Our plans miscarry because they have no aim. When a man does not know what harbor he is making for, no wind is the right wind.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Every night before going to sleep, we must ask ourselves: what weakness did I overcome today? What virtue did I acquire?
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: I shall never be ashamed of citing a bad author if the line is good.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: As is a tale, so is life: not how long it is, but how good it is, is what matters.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Life, if well lived, is long enough.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Nothing, to my way of thinking, is a better proof of a well ordered mind than a man's ability to stop just where he is and pass some time in his own company.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Associate with people who are likely to improve you. Welcome those whom you are capable of improving.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: One of the most beautiful qualities of true friendship is to understand and to be understood.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Ponder for a long time whether you shall admit a given person to your friendship; but when you have decided to admit him, welcome him with all your heart and soul.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Hang on to your youthful enthusiasms; you will be able to use them better when you are older.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Throw me to the wolves and I will return leading the pack.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Every new beginning comes from some other beginning's end.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The whole future lies in uncertainty: live immediately.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Putting things off is the biggest waste of life: it snatches away each day as it comes, and denies us the present by promising the future.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The greatest obstacle to living is expectancy, which hangs upon tomorrow and loses today.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: You are arranging what lies in Fortune's control, and abandoning what lies in yours.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Everyone hurries his life on and suffers from a yearning for the future and a weariness of the present.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: But he who bestows all of his time on his own needs, who plans out every day as if it were his last, neither longs for nor fears the morrow.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Nothing satisfies greed, but even a little satisfies nature.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: A great fortune is a great slavery.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: It is a great thing to know the season for speech and the season for silence.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: All cruelty springs from weakness.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The greatest remedy for anger is delay.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Anger, if not restrained, is frequently more hurtful to us than the injury that provokes it.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: He is most powerful who has power over himself.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Most powerful is he who has himself in his own power.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: No man was ever wise by chance.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The bravest sight in the world is to see a great man struggling against adversity.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Where fear is, happiness is not.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: There is no easy way from the earth to the stars.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Sometimes even to live is an act of courage.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Brave men rejoice in adversity, just as brave soldiers triumph in war.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Man is affected not by events but by the view he takes of them.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Men are disturbed not by things, but by the opinions which they have of things.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Some things are in our control and others not. In our control are opinion, pursuit, desire, aversion; not in our control are body, property, reputation, command.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Make the best use of what is in your power, and take the rest as it happens.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: It is not what happens to you, but how you react to it that matters.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Do not seek for things to happen the way you want them to; rather, wish that what happens happen the way it happens: then you will be happy.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Never say of anything, I have lost it; but say, I have returned it. Is your child dead? It is returned. Is your wife dead? She is returned.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: I must die. Must I then die lamenting? I must be put in chains. Must I then also lament?
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: I cannot escape death, but at least I can escape the fear of it.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Death and exile, and all other things which appear terrible, should be daily before your eyes, but chiefly death; and you will never entertain any abject thought, nor too eagerly covet anything.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Keep death and exile daily before your eyes, and you will never think anything mean, nor desire anything extravagantly.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Death is nothing terrible, else it would have appeared so to Socrates. But the terror consists in our notion of death, that it is terrible.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: When death appears as an evil, we ought immediately to remember that evils are things to be avoided, but death is inevitable.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: What is death? A tragic mask. Turn it and examine it. See, it does not bite.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: First say to yourself what you would be; and then do what you have to do.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: No man is free who is not master of himself.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Freedom is the only worthy goal in life. It is won by disregarding things that lie beyond our control.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Wealth consists not in having great possessions, but in having few wants.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: He is a wise man who does not grieve for the things which he has not, but rejoices for those which he has.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: It is impossible for a man to learn what he thinks he already knows.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: If you want to improve, be content to be thought foolish and stupid with regard to external things.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Do not explain your philosophy. Embody it.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Sheep do not throw up the grass to show the shepherds how much they have eaten; but, inwardly digesting their food, they outwardly produce wool and milk. Thus, therefore, do you likewise not show theorems to the unlearned, but the actions produced by them after they have been digested.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Only the educated are free.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: We have two ears and one mouth so that we can listen twice as much as we speak.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Circumstances do not make the man, they only reveal him to himself.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Difficulties are things that show a person what they are.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: The greater the difficulty, the more glory in surmounting it. Skillful pilots gain their reputation from storms and tempests.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: If evil be spoken of you and it be true, correct yourself; if it be a lie, laugh at it.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Any person capable of angering you becomes your master; he can anger you only when you permit yourself to be disturbed by him.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: It is the action of an uninstructed person to reproach others for his own misfortunes; of one entering upon instruction, to reproach himself; and of one perfectly instructed, to reproach neither others nor himself.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Seek not the good in external things; seek it in yourselves.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Neither should a ship rely on one small anchor, nor should life rest on a single hope.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Know, first, who you are, and then adorn yourself accordingly.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Practice yourself, for heaven's sake, in little things; and thence proceed to greater.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: No great thing is created suddenly, any more than a bunch of grapes or a fig. If you tell me that you desire a fig, I answer you that there must be time. Let it first blossom, then bear fruit, then ripen.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Remember that you are an actor in a play, the character of which is determined by the playwright. Your business is to act well the character assigned you; to choose it is another's.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: He who laughs at himself never runs out of things to laugh at.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Attach yourself to what is spiritually superior, regardless of what other people think or do. Hold to your true aspirations no matter what is going on around you.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: You may fetter my leg, but not Zeus himself can get the better of my free will.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Ask yourself: does this appearance concern the things that are within my own control or those that are not? And if it concerns anything beyond your control, be prepared to say that it is nothing to you.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: When you are going about any action, remind yourself what nature the action is. If you are going to bathe, picture to yourself the things which usually happen in the bath.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Sickness is a hindrance to the body, but not to your ability to choose, unless that is your choice.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Demand not that events should happen as you wish; but wish them to happen as they do happen, and you will go on well.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: In a word, neither death, nor exile, nor pain, nor anything of this kind is the real cause of our doing or not doing any action, but our inward opinions and principles.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Let death and exile, and all other things which appear terrible, be daily before your eyes, but death chiefly; and you will never entertain any abject thought.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: You are a little soul carrying around a corpse.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Not to be able to bear with folly is itself a kind of folly.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Nature has given men one tongue but two ears, that we may hear from others twice as much as we speak.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Preach not to others what they should eat, but eat as becomes you, and be silent.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Whoever does not regard what he has as most ample wealth is unhappy, though he be master of the world.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Never call yourself a philosopher, nor talk a great deal among the unlearned about theorems, but act conformably to them.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: If you wish to be a writer, write.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Nothing great comes into being all at once, for that is not the case even with a bunch of grapes or a fig.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: The beginning of philosophy is the recognition of the conflict between opinions.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: It is not death or pain that is to be dreaded, but the fear of pain or death.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Choose the life that is noblest, for custom can make it sweet to you.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: You will not fear death if you have learned what is truly yours: your judgment, your will, your character. All else was borrowed.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Since it is possible that death may come today, treat today as your reward: live it fully, owe it nothing.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: It is not possible to live well today unless you treat it as your last day.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: We begin to lose our hesitation to do immoral things when we lose our hesitation to speak of them.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: You will earn the respect of all if you begin by earning the respect of yourself.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: Humanity must seek what is not simple and obvious using the simple and obvious.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: In order to protect ourselves we must live like doctors and be continually treating ourselves with reason.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: If you accomplish something good with hard work, the labor passes quickly, but the good endures; if you do something shameful in pursuit of pleasure, the pleasure passes quickly, but the shame endures.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: Practice is more important than theory, for it more powerfully leads us to action.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: Virtue is not simply theoretical knowledge, but it is practical application as well.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: From good people you will learn good, but if you mingle with the bad you will destroy such soul as you had.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: Wealth is able to buy the pleasures of eating, drinking and other sensual pursuits, yet can never afford a cheerful spirit or freedom from sorrow.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: It is much more necessary to cure the soul than the body, for it is better to die than to live badly.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: Choose to die well while it is possible, lest shortly it may become necessary for you to die, but it will no longer be possible to die well.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: That each person should treat each day as if it were the last he would live, this is the way to live each day fully.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: Since every man dies, it is better to die with distinction than to live long.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: The human being is born with an inclination toward virtue.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: Most of all, the teacher of philosophy must train the soul through practice, just as the doctor trains the body.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: To relax the mind is to lose it.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: Man alone of all creatures on earth is the image of the divine.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: It is impossible for a man to learn to endure hardship who has never practiced enduring it.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: Exile does not deprive a man of anything that is truly his own: courage, justice, wisdom.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: What good are gilded rooms or precious stones fitted on the floor if you have no order in your soul?
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: Untroubled by fear of death, a person can meet every hardship in life with a steady soul.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: He who wishes to become a good man must be trained in what he must avoid and what he must pursue, like an athlete before a contest.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: Not the place, but the mind, makes exile bitter or bearable.
quotes.author: Musonius Rufus

quotes.text: Man conquers the world by conquering himself.
quotes.author: Zeno of Citium

quotes.text: Happiness is a good flow of life.
quotes.author: Zeno of Citium

quotes.text: The goal of life is living in agreement with nature.
quotes.author: Zeno of Citium

quotes.text: We have two ears and one mouth, therefore we should listen more than we speak.
quotes.author: Zeno of Citium

quotes.text: Steel your sensibilities, so that life shall hurt you as little as possible.
quotes.author: Zeno of Citium

quotes.text: No loss should be more regrettable to us than losing our time, for it is irretrievable.
quotes.author: Zeno of Citium

quotes.text: Better to trip with the feet than with the tongue.
quotes.author: Zeno of Citium

quotes.text: Wellbeing is attained by little and little, and nevertheless is no little thing itself.
quotes.author: Zeno of Citium

quotes.text: A bad feeling is a commotion of the mind repugnant to reason, and against nature.
quotes.author: Zeno of Citium

quotes.text: Fate is the endless chain of causation whereby things are; the reason or formula by which the world goes on.
quotes.author: Zeno of Citium

quotes.text: Lead me, Zeus, and you too, Destiny, wherever you have assigned me to go, and I will follow without hesitation; but even if I am disobedient and do not wish to, I shall follow no less surely.
quotes.author: Cleanthes

quotes.text: Fate guides the man who follows willingly; the man who resists, it drags.
quotes.author: Cleanthes

quotes.text: He needs little who desires but little.
quotes.author: Cleanthes

quotes.text: He has his wish, whose wish can be to have what is enough.
quotes.author: Cleanthes

quotes.text: Whatever you do, do it as if there were a witness.
quotes.author: Chrysippus

quotes.text: Living virtuously is equal to living in accordance with one's experience of the actual course of nature.
quotes.author: Chrysippus

quotes.text: The universe itself is god and the universal outpouring of its soul.
quotes.author: Chrysippus

quotes.text: There could be no justice, unless there were also injustice; no courage, unless there were cowardice; no truth, unless there were falsehood.
quotes.author: Chrysippus

quotes.text: I would rather be admired for doing right than for ruling an empire.
quotes.author: Cato the Younger

quotes.text: I begin to speak only when I am certain what I will say is not better left unsaid.
quotes.author: Cato the Younger

quotes.text: Bitter are the roots of study, but how sweet their fruit.
quotes.author: Cato the Younger

quotes.text: He is nearest to the gods who knows how to be silent, even when he is in the right.
quotes.author: Cato the Younger

quotes.text: Consider everything you have as borrowed, and Fortune the lender; the loan may be recalled at any hour, without notice.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Death is a release from all suffering, a boundary beyond which our ills cannot pass.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: He grieves more than is necessary, who grieves before it is necessary.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: What need is there to weep over parts of life? The whole of it calls for tears.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Rehearse death. To say this is to tell a person to rehearse his freedom.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: He who has learned to die has unlearned slavery; he is above any external power, or, at any rate, he is beyond it.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: It is uncertain in what place death may await you; therefore expect it in every place.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: No evil is great which is the last evil of all. Death arrives; it would be a thing to dread, if it could remain with you. But death must either not come at all, or else must come and pass away.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Death is either an end or a transition. I do not fear ceasing to be, for it is the same as never having begun.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: That day, which you fear as being the end of all things, is the birthday of your eternity.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Let us postpone nothing. Let us balance life's account every day. The greatest flaw in life is that it is always imperfect.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: He who dies has only to obey nature, which gave him life on the condition of returning it.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: You will die not because you are sick, but because you are alive.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Death you will think of as the worst of all bad things, though in fact it has nothing bad about it except the thing which comes before it, the fear of it.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: What is death but a ceasing to be what we were before? We were not, and then we came to be; we shall not be again, as we were not before our birth.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Before old age I took care to live well; in old age I take care to die well. And to die well is to die willingly.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The man who has anticipated the coming of troubles takes away their power when they arrive.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Everything, Lucilius, belongs to others; time alone is ours.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Hold every hour in your grasp. Lay hold of today's task, and you will not need to depend so much upon tomorrow's.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Nothing is more disgraceful than that an old man should have nothing to show to prove that he has lived long, except his years.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Count your years and you will be ashamed to want and to strive for the same things you wanted when you were a boy.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: It is not the last drop that empties the water clock, but all that which previously has flowed out; similarly, the final hour when we cease to exist does not of itself bring death, it merely completes the death process.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: This is our big mistake: to think we look forward to death. Most of death is already gone. Whatever time has passed is owned by death.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Let us so order our minds as if we had come to the very end. Let us postpone nothing.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: You are not master of your own time. Nature has given you enough of it, but we squander it, as though it flowed from an inexhaustible spring.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: People are frugal in guarding their personal property; but as soon as it comes to squandering time, they are most wasteful of the one thing in which it is right to be stingy.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: No one will bring back the years; no one will restore you to yourself.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Life will follow the path it began to take, and will neither reverse nor check its course. It will make no noise, it will not remind you of its swiftness.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The part of life we really live is small. For all the rest of existence is not life, but merely time.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: You must match time's swiftness with your speed in using it, and you must drink quickly as though from a rapid stream that will not always flow.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Nothing is further off than what was long ago; nothing nearer than what is coming.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Present time is very brief, so brief indeed that to some there seems to be none; for it is always in motion, it ever flows and hurries on.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The wise man will live as long as he ought, not as long as he can.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The point is, not how long you live, but how nobly you live.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: A good character, when established, is not easily overthrown.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Cling tooth and nail to the following rule: not to give in to adversity, never to trust prosperity, and always take full note of Fortune's habit of behaving just as she pleases, treating her as if she were actually going to do everything it is in her power to do.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Remember that all we have is on loan from Fortune, which can reclaim it without our permission, indeed, without even advance notice.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: He robs present ills of their power who has perceived their coming beforehand.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Everywhere is nowhere. When a person spends all his time in foreign travel, he ends by having many acquaintances, but no friends.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: It is quality rather than quantity that matters.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: To be everywhere is to be nowhere.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Each day acquire something that will fortify you against poverty, against death, indeed against other misfortunes as well.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: What progress have I made? I have begun to be a friend to myself.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The first step towards amendment is the recognition of error.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: There is no enjoying the possession of anything valuable unless one has someone to share it with.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The greatest wealth is a poverty of desires.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: A man's as miserable as he thinks he is.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Withdraw into yourself, as far as you can. Associate with those who will make a better man of you.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Only time can heal what reason cannot.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: He that does good to another does good also to himself.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Wherever there is a human being, there is an opportunity for a kindness.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: It is another's fault if he be ungrateful, but it is mine if I do not give.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: There is as much greatness of mind in acknowledging a good turn, as in doing it.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: He who receives a benefit with gratitude repays the first installment on his debt.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Nothing is more honorable than a grateful heart.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: You should live for the other person if you wish to live for yourself.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: No man was ever born wise: time teaches, if we let it, what school cannot.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Even after a bad harvest there must be sowing.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: As long as you live, keep learning how to live.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Leisure without books is death, and burial of a man alive.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: It does not matter how many books you have, but how good the books are which you have.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Nowhere is the one who is everywhere.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: If you live in harmony with nature you will never be poor; if you live according to opinion, you will never be rich.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Natural desires are limited; those which spring from false opinions have nowhere to stop.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: It is not the man who has too little who is poor, but the one who hankers after more.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Enjoy present pleasures in such a way as not to injure future ones.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The body should be treated more rigorously, that it may not be disobedient to the mind.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: We are members of one great body, planted by nature. We must consider that we were born for the good of the whole.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Man is a reasoning animal.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: While we teach, we learn.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Every day, therefore, should be regulated as if it were the one that brings up the rear, the one that rounds out and completes our lives.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Let us go to our sleep with joy and gladness; let us say: I have lived; the course which Fortune set for me is finished.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: He who has lived a day has lived an age.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Believe me, it is the sign of a great man, and one who is above human error, to allow no part of his time to be skimmed off.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Life is divided into three periods: that which has been, that which is, that which will be. Of these the present time is short, the future is doubtful, the past is certain.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: They live as if they were destined to live forever; no thought of their frailty ever enters their heads.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: You will hear many men saying: after my fiftieth year I shall retire into leisure. And what guarantee, pray, have you that your life will last longer?
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: How stupid to forget our mortality, and put off sensible plans to our fiftieth and sixtieth years, aiming to begin life from a point at which few have arrived.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The greatest hindrance to living is expectancy, which depends upon the morrow and wastes today.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Nothing lasts forever, few things even last for long: all are susceptible of decay in one way or another; moreover all that begins also ends.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Whatever begins, also ends.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Time discovers truth.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Every reason for hope is also a reason for fear.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Cease to hope and you will cease to fear.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Hope and fear march in step; both belong to a mind in suspense, hanging on what tomorrow may bring.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The mind should be kept in the present. Fear looks to the future, regret to the past; the present alone is where courage lives.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Look at the vastness of time behind you, and at another infinity of time before you. In this immensity, what difference is there between a life of three days and one of three generations?
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: In a little while you will have forgotten everything; in a little while everything will have forgotten you.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Human life: its duration, a point; its substance, flowing; its perception, dim; the fabric of the whole body, corruptible; its fortune, hard to divine; its fame, uncertain.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Yesterday a drop of semen, tomorrow a handful of ashes or spice.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Constantly bring to mind those who have been through great changes of fortune. Where are they all now? Smoke and ash, and a story told, or not even a story.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Think continually how many physicians are dead after often knitting their brows over the sick; how many astrologers after predicting the deaths of others; how many philosophers after endless discourses on death or immortality.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: All of us are creatures of a day; the rememberer and the remembered alike.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Hippocrates, after curing many diseases, himself fell sick and died. So too the Chaldeans, who foretold the deaths of many, were themselves seized by fate.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Soon the earth will cover us all. Then the earth itself will change, and what comes of that will change again, and again without end.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Where are those men now who pitched their tents in fame? Nowhere. And the things they strained for? Vapor, dust, a tale, or less than a tale.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Camillus, Caeso, Volesus, Dentatus: all dead. And soon those who remember them will be dead too, and those who remembered the rememberers.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Give yourself a gift: the present moment.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Remind yourself that past and future have no power over you. Only the present, and even that can be minimized. Just mark off its limits.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Forget everything else. Keep hold of this alone and remember it: each of us lives only now, this brief instant. The rest has been lived already, or is impossible to see.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Were you to live three thousand years, or even a countless multiple of that, keep in mind that no one ever loses a life other than the one they are living.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: It is one of the acts of life, this act by which we die; it is sufficient then in this act also to do well what we have in hand.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Await death with a cheerful mind, as nothing else than a dissolution of the elements of which every living being is compounded.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: If the elements themselves suffer nothing by their perpetual conversion into one another, why should a man have any apprehension about the change and dissolution of them all? For it is according to nature, and nothing is evil which is according to nature.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: He who has a vehement desire for posthumous fame does not consider that every one of those who remember him will himself also die very soon.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: What is even an eternal remembrance? A mere nothing. What then is that about which we ought to employ our serious pains? This one thing: thoughts just, and acts social, and words which never lie.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Deem not life a thing of consequence. For look at the yawning void of the future, and at that other limitless space, the past.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: In the life of a man, his time is but a moment, his being an incessant flux, his senses a dim rushlight, his body a prey of worms, his soul an unquiet eddy, his fortune dark, and his fame doubtful.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: What then is there which still detains you here, if the objects of sense are easily changed and never stand still, and the organs of perception are dull and easily receive false impressions?
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Wait for death with a cheerful mind; for this is according to nature, and nothing is evil which is according to nature.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Do not despise death, but be well content with it, since this too is one of those things which nature wills.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: As it is with a summer fig: now ripe, now past. As it is with life: now here, now remembered, now forgotten.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Perhaps the desire of the thing called fame torments you. See how soon everything is forgotten, and look at the chaos of infinite time on each side of the present.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Some things are hurrying into existence, and others are hurrying out of it; and of that which is coming into existence part is already extinguished.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: The world is mere change, and this life, opinion.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: How many after being celebrated by fame have been given up to oblivion; and how many who have celebrated the fame of others have long been dead.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Whatever is in any way beautiful has its source of beauty in itself, and is complete in itself; praise forms no part of it.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Everything material soon disappears in the substance of the whole; and everything formal is very soon taken back into the universal reason.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Near is your forgetfulness of all things; and near the forgetfulness of you by all.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Always observe how ephemeral and worthless human things are, and what was yesterday a little mucus, tomorrow will be a mummy or ashes.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Consider yourself to be dead, and to have completed your life up to the present time; and live according to nature the remainder which is allowed you.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Love only that which happens to you and is spun with the thread of your destiny. For what is more suitable?
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Everything harmonizes with me, which is harmonious to you, O Universe. Nothing for me is too early nor too late, which is in due time for you.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: All that you see will soon perish, and those who witness this perishing will soon perish themselves.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: He who dies at the extremity of old age will be brought to the same condition with him who dies prematurely.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Think of the whole of existence, of which you are the smallest part; think of the whole of time, in which a brief and momentary portion has been allotted to you.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: This is mortality's lesson: the present is all you can give up, because the present is all you have.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Loss is change, and change is nature's joy. Since the world began, all has proceeded by change, and to the end of time it will be so.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Nature gives all and takes all back. To her the man educated into humility says: give what you will; take back what you will. And he says this in no proud defiance, but in obedience and goodwill.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Spend your brief moment then according to nature, and pass on your way content, just as an olive falls when it is ripe, blessing nature who produced it and thanking the tree on which it grew.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: You have embarked, you have made the voyage, you have come to shore: get out. If to another life, there is no want of gods, not even there. But if to a state without sensation, you will cease to be held by pains and pleasures.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Why should any of these things which happen externally so much distract you? Give yourself leisure to learn something new and good, and cease to be whirled around.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Now there remains only for you to reflect that death is not to be feared: it is a function of nature, and to a reasoning being nothing natural is evil.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: It is not death that we should fear, but we should fear never having lived at all, and never having awakened to live rightly.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: What springs from earth dissolves to earth again, and heaven-born things fly to their native seat.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Man, you have been a citizen in this great state, the world; what difference does it make to you whether for five years or three? For that which is conformable to the laws is just for all.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Depart then satisfied, for he also who releases you is satisfied.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: When a man has done you any wrong, immediately consider with what opinion about good or evil he has done wrong. For when you have seen this, you will pity him, and will neither wonder nor be angry.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Men exist for the sake of one another. Teach them then or bear with them.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: The best way of avenging yourself is not to become like the wrongdoer.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: It is the privilege of human nature to love those that disoblige us.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Kindness is invincible, if it be sincere, not fawning nor pretense.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Do not be ashamed of help, for it is your task to achieve your assigned duty, like a soldier in a storming party.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: That which is really beautiful has no need of anything; not more than law, not more than truth, not more than benevolence or modesty.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: If any man is able to convince me and show me that I do not think or act right, I will gladly change; for I seek the truth, by which no man was ever injured.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Everything that exists is in a manner the seed of that which will be.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Nothing has such power to broaden the mind as the ability to investigate systematically and truly all that comes under your observation in life.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Let opinion be taken away, and no man will think himself wronged. If no man shall think himself wronged, then is there no more any such thing as wrong.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: The act of dying is one of the acts of life.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Look at everything that exists, and observe that it is already in dissolution and in change, and as it were putrefaction or dispersion, or that everything is so constituted by nature as to die.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: How small a part of the boundless and unfathomable time is assigned to every man; for it is very soon swallowed up in the eternal.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Every part of me will be reduced by change into some part of the universe, and that again will change into another part of the universe, and so on forever.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Frightened of change? But what can exist without it? What is closer to nature's heart? Can you take a hot bath and leave the firewood as it was?
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Nature takes substance and makes a horse. Like a sculptor with wax. And then melts it down and uses the material for a tree. Then for a person. Then for something else.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: We were born to work together like feet, hands, and eyes, like the two rows of teeth, upper and lower.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: What injures the hive injures the bee.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: Epictetus was a slave, Marcus Aurelius an emperor; philosophy made them equals before death.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Memento mori: remember that you must die, so that you remember to live.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: The Stoics kept an image of death on the table not to darken the feast, but to sweeten it.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Do not fear death; fear the unlived life.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: While I thought that I was learning how to live, I have been learning how to die.
quotes.author: Attributed to Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Death twitches my ear. Live, he says; I am coming.
quotes.author: Virgil

quotes.text: It is not death that is bitter, but the fear of death that poisons the living hour.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: To philosophize is to learn how to die.
quotes.author: Cicero, after the Stoics

quotes.text: The whole life of a philosopher is a preparation for death.
quotes.author: Cicero, after the Stoics

quotes.text: No one can have a peaceful life who thinks too much about lengthening it.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: He that fears death loses the life he has.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Every hour of every day, countless mortals complete their journey. Their road is your road; walk it well.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: You are dying every minute you are alive; spend those minutes on what death cannot take.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: The door is always open. Until you must walk through it, do your work.
quotes.author: After Epictetus

quotes.text: When you kiss your child goodnight, whisper to yourself: tomorrow you may die. This is not morbid; it is how you learn to kiss them properly.
quotes.author: After Epictetus

quotes.text: What harm is there while you are kissing your child to whisper softly: tomorrow you will die?
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: As a ship's captain caught in harbor by a storm waits for the sea to settle, so the wise man waits on death: neither hurrying toward it nor fleeing from it.
quotes.author: After Seneca

quotes.text: Live among men as if god beheld you; speak to god as if men were listening.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: It is the power of the mind to be unconquerable.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Valor withers without adversity.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The willing, destiny guides them; the unwilling, destiny drags them.
quotes.author: Cleanthes, quoted by Seneca

quotes.text: Life is a play: what matters is not how long the acting lasts, but how good the acting is.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: It matters not how long you live, but how well; and often the how-well lies in not living long.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Death is the wish of some, the relief of many, and the end of all.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The day which we fear as our last is but the birthday of eternity.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The final hour is only one of many hours; each of the others also carried you toward death.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Whatever years lie behind us are in death's hands.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: Let us cherish and love old age; for it is full of pleasure if one knows how to use it.
quotes.author: Seneca

quotes.text: The unschooled man blames others when things go badly; the beginner blames himself; the wise man blames no one.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Keep company with those who make you better.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: If you would be a reader, read; if a writer, write.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Give me by all means the shorter and nobler life, instead of one that is longer but of less account.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: A ship should not ride on a single anchor, nor life on a single hope.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: First learn the meaning of what you say, and then speak.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: It takes more than just a good looking body. You have got to have the heart and soul to go with it.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: To accuse others for one's own misfortunes is a sign of want of education. To accuse oneself shows that one's education has begun. To accuse neither oneself nor others shows that one's education is complete.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Don't just say you have read books. Show that through them you have learned to think better, to be a more discriminating and reflective person.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: The key is to keep company only with people who uplift you, whose presence calls forth your best.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: If anyone tells you that a certain person speaks ill of you, do not make excuses about what is said of you, but answer: he was ignorant of my other faults, else he would not have mentioned these alone.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Whoever then wishes to be free, let him neither wish for anything nor avoid anything which depends on others.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Men are not afraid of things, but of how they view them.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: The essence of philosophy is that a man should so live that his happiness shall depend as little as possible on external things.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: God has entrusted me with myself. No man is free who is not master of himself.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: Remind yourself that what you love is mortal, that what you love is not your own. It is granted to you for the present while, and not irrevocably, nor forever.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: In the presence of death, learn what your judgments are worth: if they hold there, they will hold anywhere.
quotes.author: After Epictetus

quotes.text: As you would not sail past your harbor, do not sail past your life waiting for a better wind.
quotes.author: After Seneca

quotes.text: He who postpones the hour of living rightly is like the rustic who waits for the river to run out before he crosses.
quotes.author: Horace, echoing the Stoics

quotes.text: Believe that each day that dawns on you is your last; the hour you never hoped for will prove a happy surprise.
quotes.author: Horace, echoing the Stoics

quotes.text: Seize the day, trusting as little as possible to tomorrow.
quotes.author: Horace, echoing the Stoics

quotes.text: Pale death knocks with impartial foot at the hovels of the poor and the towers of kings.
quotes.author: Horace, echoing the Stoics

quotes.text: Think of yourself as a torch bearer in a relay: you did not light the flame, you will not carry it to the end, but while it is in your hand, run well.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: The fear of death follows from the fear of life. A man who lives fully is prepared to die at any time.
quotes.author: Attributed to Stoic tradition

quotes.text: What upsets people is not things themselves but their judgments about the things.
quotes.author: Epictetus

quotes.text: When walking, walk. When eating, eat. When dying, die.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Each night, lay down your day as a completed thing. If morning comes, it is a gift; if not, you finished well.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: You cannot control the length of your life, but you can control its width and depth.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Every farewell rehearses the final one; make each parting kind.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: The dead outnumber the living, and every generation joins them. This is not tragedy; this is the procession you were born into.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Do not count on tomorrow to begin virtue; tomorrow belongs to no one.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: A candle does not mourn its melting; it gives light.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Ask each evening: if this was the last day, was it worthy? Correct tomorrow what the answer shames.
quotes.author: After Seneca

quotes.text: The grave has no calendar; it receives the young and the old on the same terms.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Fortune can take your goods, your titles, your body. Your character travels with you to the end, and it is the only luggage death allows.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Live each day so that you need neither fear tomorrow nor blush for yesterday.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: He is not poor who has little, but he who desires much; he is not rich who has much, but he who needs nothing more.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Time is the one loan that cannot be repaid; spend it as a dying man would, for that is what you are.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: The fig tree does not ask for more summers; it ripens the fruit of this one.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Death is not the opposite of life, but the frame that gives the picture its meaning.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: What the returning wave takes, the sea gave. Grieve if you must, but do not call the sea a thief.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: You did not exist for an eternity before your birth and it caused you no pain; the eternity after will be the same.
quotes.author: After Seneca

quotes.text: The man who has crossed many rivers is not afraid of the last ford.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Look at the stars and remember: their light left them long ago, yet still it serves. So may your works outrun your life.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Practice losing small things cheerfully: a cup, a coin, an hour. You are in training for losing everything gracefully.
quotes.author: After Epictetus

quotes.text: Begin each day by telling yourself: today I shall meet with interference, ingratitude, insolence, disloyalty, ill-will, and selfishness, all of them due to the offender's ignorance of what is good and evil.
quotes.author: Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: When you wake in the morning, remember what a privilege it is to be alive: to breathe, to think, to enjoy, to love. Some who slept beside you in this world did not wake.
quotes.author: After Marcus Aurelius

quotes.text: The clock of your life was wound once, and no hand can wind it again. When it stops, let it be found still telling the true hour.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Death is a debt we all must pay; the wise pay it without complaint, for they had the use of the loan.
quotes.author: After Euripides, adopted by the Stoics

quotes.text: No one is so old that he may not honestly hope for one more day, and no one so young that he may count on it.
quotes.author: After Cicero

quotes.text: The shortness of life should not sadden us, but spur us: a runner does not weep that the track is short; he runs.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Leave the apple sweeter for your bite; leave the world better for your living. Then depart as a guest who has dined well.
quotes.author: After Epicurus, honored by the Stoics

quotes.text: It is not that the hour is late; it is that you slept through the morning. Rise now; the day is still yours.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Each man's days are numbered, but no man knows the number: therefore treat every number as if it were the last, and none as if it did not count.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Do today what your deathbed self will thank you for.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: A life spent fearing death is a death endured twice; a life spent well needs to die only once.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: The river does not stop at the pleasant meadow, nor time at your happiest hour. Drink where you stand.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Fear not that your life shall come to an end, but rather fear that it shall never have a beginning.
quotes.author: John Henry Newman, echoing the Stoics

quotes.text: What you do every day matters more than what you do once in a while, and death is the reason why.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: The hourglass never argues; it only empties. Be as honest with your hours as it is.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Even the longest life is short against eternity; even the shortest is long enough for virtue.
quotes.author: After Seneca

quotes.text: He who would be brave, let him remember he will die; he who would be humble, let him remember the same.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Your ancestors survived plagues, wars, and famines so that you might have this single day. Do not spend it complaining.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: The reed that bends outlives the oak that resists; but both, in the end, return to the soil. Bend, stand, and be at peace with the returning.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: You are not late. You are not early. You are mortal, and the time is now.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Of all that you will leave behind, only this is truly yours to shape: the kind of person you were.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Treat the interruption as the appointment: fate keeps its own calendar.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Nothing is more common than an unlived life carried to the grave by a body that survived to old age.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Grief for the dead is love with nowhere to go; direct it to the living, who can still receive it.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: The best time to plant a tree was twenty years ago; the second-best time is today. Death is why there is no third-best time.
quotes.author: Proverb, in the Stoic spirit

quotes.text: When death finds you, let it find you alive.
quotes.author: African proverb, in the Stoic spirit

quotes.text: We are all under the same sentence, only with indefinite reprieves; the difference between us is what we do while we wait.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: Take the cold plunge, write the letter, say the words: the dead envy you these small braveries.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: An examined death makes an examined life; look at your end until it stops looking back.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition

quotes.text: The purpose of remembering death is not to darken life but to light it: nothing burns brighter than a flame that knows it is brief.
quotes.author: Stoic tradition
